data.title: Tesla: Clear Skies In 2026, But A Storm Could Cut Valuation In 2027
data.description:
It has been a long journey since January 2015, when I first covered Tesla, Inc. (TSLA). With the stock at a split-adjusted ~$12/share, I argued that short-selling the brand was a mistake and that Tesla was instead a contrarian Buy. We all know how that turned out: a precarious startup in electric cars transformed into an S&P 500 (SP500) juggernaut, with a market capitalization of $1.39T. It is easy to overlook that in 2015, similarly to today, Tesla had a valuation that seemed out of touch with reality. At the time, there were many calls to short the company, and my first article was primarily predicated on the argument that shorting a brand that is executing on a dream is potentially a disastrous financial decision.

I didn't cover Tesla again until April 2024, again rating the stock a Buy, although I was perplexed by how far it had fallen and wondered aloud if I should have hit the Sell button. However, I accepted the vicissitudes of the market and found a reason to be bullish again, upgrading to Strong Buy after test driving full self-driving, or FSD, on my Model 3. However, this time I was not lulled to sleep by dreams of unsustainable gains; instead, I rang a bell at the top when I took profits and downgraded Tesla to Hold as the stock rallied to $463/share, arguing in Dec. 2024 that "rapid growth is priced in."

In the year or so since, Tesla stock has fallen by 7% versus a 13% increase in the S&P 500, so what is now priced into the stock? The investment thesis in Tesla is complicated by the company's complexity. To answer this question, we cannot evaluate Tesla as a single entity. Instead, it must be seen as a sum of its parts, including an automotive business, an energy business, and a growth portfolio, consisting of full self-driving, Robotaxi, and Optimus.

Writing this article, I initially expected that I would be bearish on Tesla. With FSD and Robotaxi not contributing much to earnings and Optimus a lofty growth driver in the distant future, I was expecting to find growth targets in 2026 that could not be met without a massive Robotaxi roll-out. Surprisingly, this is not the case: in fact, short- and medium-term targets seem very attainable.

While Tesla's valuation around $435/share appears to leave no margin for error, the analyst community has back-loaded growth, leaving the company with a very strong chance to meet consensus estimates in the coming quarter and year. With respect to the upcoming Q4 2025 earnings (January 28th), it is more likely that the stock will rally than crash, owing to reasonable estimates that will be discussed in the section below. However, investors are paying up-front for a revolution that has not happened yet.

Thus, my cautious stance is to rate Tesla stock a Hold. To put new money to work, lower prices or faster execution on Robotaxi/FSD are required. However, I consider this cautious stance to be short-term bullish as softness in shares and low expectations set the stage wherein the next leg of the stock is more likely up than down.

Tesla: A Sum of the Parts Valuation

To understand Tesla as a business, we must first separate and analyze its segments. Below is a table from Tesla's Q3 2025 10-Q report; it is somewhat counterintuitive that FSD revenue is counted within the automotive sales segment. From the report, the exact monthly revenue of FSD is unclear, although Tesla reports $3.83B in deferred revenue from FSD (among other things), increasing from $3.60B in Q4 2024. This reinforces the conclusion that FSD recurring revenue has not become material enough to warrant its own line item. Recently, Musk disclosed the FSD will only be available as a $99/month subscription as of February, rather than a possible $8,000 standalone purchase.

The "services and other" segment is also slightly counterintuitive, again because it does not include the SaaS contribution of FSD. Instead, it includes non-warranty services, used vehicle sales, supercharging, and insurance.

Therefore, for the purpose of our sum of the parts, Tesla's business is sorted into three segments:

  1. The Legacy Auto Business (Including FSD): Tesla's automotive business is a mature cash cow, facing cyclical headwinds. This includes automotive sales ($20.35B), regulatory credits ($417M), services and other ($3.48B), and automotive leasing ($429M). The total legacy business is therefore $24.7B, or 87.8% of revenues. YoY, this segment grew by 8.3%.
  2. The Energy Business: A fast-growing juggernaut that has quietly become Tesla's growth engine. This includes energy generation and storage sales ($3.28B) and energy generation and storage leasing ($134M). In total, the energy business achieved $3.41B in Q3 sales, or 12.2%, while expanding quickly at 43.5% YoY.
  3. The Future Business: Robotaxi and Optimus. Products that promise billions but remain in the early stages of monetization. They are not yet contributors to Tesla's sales.

With these three segments in mind, let us consider their value. To begin, it is impressive that despite slowing deliveries and declining automotive credits, the legacy business was still able to swing to 8.3% YoY growth in Q3. The legacy auto business run rate is approximately $83.97B (87.8% of TTM revenue). To assign a value, I used the most optimistic possible assumption: imagine that Tesla's automotive business were priced in the most favorable manner in the automotive industry. That would be Ferrari (RACE), which trades for 7.14x 2025 sales. This admittedly optimistic assumption places the legacy auto business at a value of approximately $600B.

Next, consider the energy business. At 12.2% of current revenues, this implies a run rate of $11.66B. A generous 8x sales multiple would imply a valuation of $93B.

Finally, we have Robotaxi and Optimus. These are essentially impossible to value except in terms of total addressable market (TAM). Let us make the very optimistic assumption that Robotaxi is commercialized beginning in 2027 and secures sufficient market share to justify an equivalent market capitalization to Uber (UBER), the largest ride-hailing company in the world. This would value Robotaxi at $175B, with the remaining value of Tesla's shares coming from Optimus. While the TAM for Optimus should be massive, its mass production has been delayed until 2027 but almost certainly will require until 2028 or later.

Optimus, together with Robotaxi, supplies a "growth premium" that represents a substantial part of Tesla's market capitalization at 50.1%. In total, even with highly optimistic assumptions for the legacy business, half of Tesla's valuation is entirely theoretical, based on products that have yet to launch.

Business Segment* ~TTM Revenue Valuation Multiple Implied Value Value per Share Percent Value
Legacy Auto & Services $83.97B 7.14x Sales $599.5B $186 43.2%
Energy $11.66B 8.0x Sales $93B $29 6.7%
Robotaxi -- -- $175B $54 12.5%
Optimus -- -- $522.5B $162 37.6%
Total $1,390B $431 100%

*Tesla: Sum of the Parts Valuation - Author's Estimate.

Now we have sized up Tesla and approximated what investors are paying for each segment. The purpose is to evaluate whether shares are a good purchase at the current market price. Some bulls may argue that Robotaxi will eclipse Uber, or that Optimus will eclipse the legacy auto business. That is possible, and if you think so, the table above provides a good reference for how that could add value to the stock. As I said in January 2015, Tesla's future is very uncertain, and that could be quite bullish.

Tesla: Outlook over the Short, Medium, and Long Term

Nevertheless, these future values will require vast growth. The further this growth lies in the future, the larger it will need to be as an appropriate discount rate is applied.

Here is how the business will be judged in the short, medium, and long term:

Short Term (Q4 2025 Earnings on 1/28/26): The bar for the next earnings report is low. With consensus delivery estimates falling to ~418k vehicles, the market is bracing for a miss. However, analysts have set revenue targets for the quarter of only $24.75B, 3.7% below the $25.71B achieved in Q4 2024. Given growth in the energy segment, these numbers are achievable, perhaps even conservative.

On January 2nd, data was released showing 418,227 vehicles were delivered in Q4 2025, a 15.6% YoY decline versus 495,570 in Q4 2024. This stark number is why estimates have declined and presumably why the stock has moved lower in recent weeks. In Q4 2024, Tesla sold $20.0B of autos, an ASP of $43,200. This would imply $18.06B in sales in Q4 2025.

However, the other item confirmed by the January 2nd release was that energy deployed 14.2 GWh of storage products in Q4. This is a massive surge since, according to the Q3 2025 10-Q, "32.5 GWh of energy storage products [were sold] through the third quarter." Thus, in the first 9 months of 2025, 32.5 GWh corresponded to $8.55B in sales. Correspondingly, we should expect $3.74B in Q4 2024. Let's imagine that the other smaller segments of Tesla's sales were either constant from Q3 or the average of performance for the first 9 months of 2025 (whichever is lower).

This leads to the following estimate for Q4 2025:

Total Revenues $25,827 $28,095
Tesla: Q4 2025 Predictions by Segment* Q4 2025 Q3 2025
Automotive Sales $18,060 $20,359
Automotive Regulatory Credits $417 $417
Energy Generation and Storage Sales $3,740 $3,281
Services and Other $3,053 $3,475
Total Revenue from Sales and Services $25,270 $27,532
Automotive Leasing $429 $429
Energy Generation and Storage Leasing $128 $134

*Tesla's Q4 2025 Performance - Author's Estimate.

In Q4, analysts are expecting $24.75B in revenue, leading to $94.66B for the full year. However, with $69.93B in sales through Q3, our estimate of $25.83B would represent a 4.4% beat on the top line for Q4 and $95.76B for 2025, a slight beat of 1.2%.

My conclusion is that in the short term, Tesla is positioned for a modest beat when it reports earnings on Jan. 28th. However, the legacy auto business is still shrinking versus a year ago. In Q4 2024, Tesla reported $25.71B in sales; thus, if the estimate above proves correct, sales will be flat YoY. The energy business is effectively masking weakness in the auto business. With the stock down 12.2% since all-time highs in Dec., I am moderately bullish, expecting the stock to stabilize in the coming quarter and perhaps trend higher.

Tesla: Analysts Projections for Revenue Growth in the Medium to Long Term

Medium Term (2026): This year, I had assumed that Tesla needed a huge Robotaxi launch to meet growth estimates. However, analysts are anticipating $107.4B in sales, a 13.5% growth rate. This is achievable with energy and FSD sustaining growth, with the automotive business stabilizing. A Robotaxi revolution is not being priced into 2026 estimates by the analyst community.

Based on our estimates for Q4, energy would reach $12.29B in 2025 sales. If its 43.5% YoY growth continues, this segment can reach $17.64B in 2026 sales. This is $5.35B in new revenue. Services & others have grown in a stable fashion, at a 25% growth rate on 2025 revenue of $14B; a contribution of $3.5B in new revenue is achievable.

Where things get somewhat more difficult is that the legacy auto business needs to stabilize in the next 12 months and return to growth in order to meet analyst estimates in 2026. Analysts see $107.39B in revenues, or $11.63B in new revenue. With energy supplying $5.35B and services $3.5B, $2.78B in growth is required for the legacy auto business. This, in a nutshell, is why my outlook for Tesla gets cloudier the further into the future we go. The math says we either need a turnaround in the auto business to justify analyst growth projection or another catalyst, such as accelerated Robotaxi deployment, will be needed to distract the market from the near-term underperformance.

Long Term (2027+): This is where the math becomes very hard, with 2027 and 2028 consensus estimates demanding growth to re-accelerate by 17% and 21%, respectively. This growth cannot mathematically be satisfied by cars, energy, or FSD. It requires a successful launch of a Robotaxi fleet. By 2028, Tesla will need to add $57.7B in revenue, eclipsing Uber's (UBER) 2025 sales. For comparison, Elon Musk's pay package had the goal of 10M FSD subscriptions. This sounds immense; however, $99/month for 10M subscriptions is $11.88B per year. Perhaps that could help plug the hole in 2026 if subscription growth expands, but it is woefully insufficient in 2027 and massively low beyond that. Long-term buyers are betting that execution on Robotaxi will come through next year.

After 2028, Optimus has to kick in to turbocharge growth further. Based on DCF valuation, Tesla is likely worth $276/share, assuming 14.4% revenue growth for 5 years, 10.7% until year 20, and 3% growth in perpetuity. It is impossible to believe the company is not overvalued by 50% at the present time, and sooner or later, Optimus and Robotaxi will need to deliver, or else the stock will decline substantially.

Conclusions

Tesla is a polarizing stock, perhaps more so than almost any stock on the market today. This is because bears see a shrinking automotive business in cyclical decline, propped up (for now) by huge growth in energy that still cannot fully justify the current market price. Bulls, on the other hand, see a huge first-mover advantage in robotics and ride-hailing with Optimus and Robotaxi potentially supplying a huge TAM once their respective commercialization roll-outs gain steam.

I, on the other hand, am caught in between. I have been captivated by Tesla's fantastic progress in FSD; however, I am unsure if this will lead to true unassisted driving. In addition, Tesla has specified that a substantial reduction in the cost of production is required for optimal unit economics for Robotaxi. Clearly, there are many challenges that must be overcome. Optimus remains a "blue-sky" opportunity, and we don't know what the demand will be for a $20-$30k humanoid robot - even if Tesla can make good on its production timeline.

For the time being, my outlook on Tesla is a Hold rating with a bullish short-term bias. In Q4 2025, analyst estimates are likely too low, setting up a potential beat on earnings next week. However, the further into the future we go, the more cloudy the outlook becomes. Investors who want upside in AI, robotics, and robotaxis may be attracted to Tesla at these prices; however, they should remember that further upside is speculative at these levels.

data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Optimism Retracts
data.description:
Optimism among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks decreased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, both neutral sentiment and pessimism increased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, decreased 6.3 percentage points to 43.2%. Bullish sentiment is above its historical average of 37.5% for the eighth time in 11 weeks.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, increased 1.8 percentage points to 24.1%. Neutral sentiment is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 79th time in 81 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, increased 4.5 percentage points to 32.7%. Bearish sentiment is above its historical average of 31.0% for the 47th time in 52 weeks.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) decreased 10.8 percentage points to 10.4%. The bull-bear spread is above its historical average of 6.5% for the 12th time in 51 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members what their performance forecast for the S&P 500 index is in 2026.

Here is how they responded:

  • Up 10% or more: 25.6%
  • Up between 2% and 9%: 43.5%
  • Flat between +1% and –1%: 12.1%
  • Down between 2% and 9%: 10.3%
  • Down 10% or more: 7.2%

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 43.2%, down 6.3 percentage points
  • Neutral: 24.1%, up 1.8 percentage points
  • Bearish: 32.7%, up 4.5 percentage points

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%
  • Neutral: 31.5%
  • Bearish: 31.0%

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987.

data.title: Alexandria Real Estate Equities: Increased Pain Requires A Downgrade
data.description:
One company going through a really tough time right now is Alexandria Real Estate Equities, Inc. (ARE). For those not familiar with the business, it operates as a REIT that focuses on renting out real estate to the life sciences industry. Conceptually, this is an industry that should do well in the long run. After all, the population of the U.S. and the world continues to grow. And on top of this, major investments in life sciences should create more demand for real estate tied to it. However, there have been certain challenges that the company has faced as of late. And those have started to materialize in the form of lower revenue and cash flows. This is disappointing to say the least. The weakness the company has exhibited has been enough to push the stock down considerably. In fact, since I called it a Strong Buy candidate back in October of last year, the stock has dropped 26.1%. That's at a time when the S&P 500 (SP500) has increased 4.5%.

Needless to say, I am unhappy about that development. And it looks as though further pain could lie ahead in the near term. Because of this, I do think that downgrading the stock to a Buy makes sense here. Having said that, a further downgrade is unlikely because of the overall business model and how seriously management is taking these challenges. Naturally, the picture could change as new data comes in. And that data is expected to come out after the market closes on January 26th. But absent something major and unexpected coming out of the woodwork, I believe that a Buy rating will prove sufficient.

Checking in on Alexandria Real Estate Equities

The life sciences industry is massive and offers tremendous potential for any firm that operates in any segment of it. At the forefront of this from a real estate standpoint has been Alexandria Real Estate Equities, a player who owns and leases out its real estate specifically for the industry. But the firm doesn't own just any real estate. It has, over the years, pushed what it calls its "megacampus" platform. As the image above illustrates, this is essentially a complex dedicated to the life sciences industry. The idea here is to centralize assets in order to improve operational and other synergies. And according to management, about 77% of the annual rental revenue that it generates comes from this business model.

This is incredibly appealing to major firms in the life sciences industry. This is evidenced by the fact that the occupancy rate of its megacampus assets stands at a hefty 91%. That compares to the 73% average of the market more broadly, creating an occupancy outperformance rate of 18%. That is immense. And if everything goes well, we should expect this overall trend to continue. In addition to the U.S. population continuing to grow, there's the fact that a large chunk of the population, which is going to see even more issues as it ages, suffers from major health-related issues. 129 million adults in the U.S., for instance, suffer from one or more chronic diseases. One in five deaths is caused by heart disease. 40% of individuals will be diagnosed with some form of cancer during their lifetimes. And right now, there are 7.2 million adults in the U.S. over the age of 65 who have Alzheimer's.

Given the fact that the population will continue to grow, we should expect additional investments to be made in the life sciences industry. But unfortunately, financial performance for the business has been underwhelming as of late. During the most recent quarter, which is the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, revenue for the company amounted to $751.9 million. That's actually a drop from the $791.6 million reported the previous year. This decline of 5% seems to have been driven largely by asset sales. Recognizing that competition is rising in this space, management has sold off non-core assets. And according to the company, if we were to exclude asset sales that were completed after January 1st of 2024, total revenue would have been essentially flat in the most recent quarter compared to the same quarter the year before.

According to management, from the start of the 2025 fiscal year through October of 2025, the business had completed $508 million worth of asset sales. It also had pending asset sales amounting to $1.03 billion. That is specifically its share of it. That does not mean that management is not pushing for other developments. At the end of the most recent quarter, the company possessed 39.11 million square feet of space, 37.30 million of which was operating. The other 1.81 million involved properties being held for sale. However, the firm also had 969,524 square feet of property under development that would be completed between 2025 and 2026. And for 2027 and beyond, it had another 2.80 million square feet worth under construction. In fact, when it comes specifically to the megacampus strategy that the business has, it intends to spend about $4.9 billion to complete it. For an enterprise with a market capitalization now of $9.05 billion, that is massive.

The drop in revenue for the business brought with it a decline in profitability as well. Net income went from $164.7 million to negative $234.9 million. Operating cash flow dropped from $477.3 million to $433.5 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $445.5 million to $418.9 million. FFO, or funds from operations, dropped from $400.3 million down to $371.2 million. And on an adjusted basis, the decline was from $407.9 million to $377.8 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business contracted from $554.9 million to $532.5 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. Once again, revenue, profits, and cash flows all contracted. Asset sales will do that. But I would be derelict in my duties if I ignored the fact that the company has acknowledged real industry weakness. Although the megacampus approach that the company has should be a major differentiator that should justify higher occupancy rates moving forward, the fact of the matter is that the last few years have seen a boom in investing when it comes to life sciences. This is because, during the COVID-19 pandemic, remote work became more popular. This meant that traditional office assets experienced higher vacancy rates. Out of necessity, some of those decided to convert to life sciences properties. And that has, without a doubt, proven to be an issue for the business.

In the image above, you can see what has happened in terms of market inventory as measured by millions of square feet in the top three markets that Alexandria Real Estate Equities operates in. The firm went from operating in markets with a combined 71.4 million square feet in 2020 to 107.4 million square feet by the third quarter of 2025. The availability of these properties increased also, putting downward pressure on property owners and developers alike. A continuation of this trend will absolutely negatively affect a firm such as Alexandria Real Estate Equities. And the important thing is that management needs to have an answer to this distress.

Management has made a couple of changes. Over the years, as life science demand has dropped significantly, falling from 7.7 million square feet needed in the top three markets in 2020 to 5.3 million square feet in the third quarter of 2025, the firm has prioritized maintaining its overall leverage. Although there has been a slight increase, the company continues to have a net leverage ratio of between 5.5 and 6.

That's not terribly far off from the pre-pandemic year of 5.7. But in order to really get its affairs where they need to be, the company has even reduced annual construction spending. The average annual spending between 2021 and 2023 for the business was about $2.8 billion. But for the 2024 through 2026 fiscal years, that has been projected to be about $1.1 billion lower, or about $1.7 billion annually.

Management is also reducing capital expenditure funding needs for 2027 and the years thereafter. This has included making changes to its construction strategy, as well as looking at opportunities to divest of assets. The goal here is to achieve $240 million of incremental annual net operating income from 2027 and beyond deliveries. For 2025 in its entirety, management said that completed and pending asset sales amounted to $1.8 billion. That's quite a bit higher than the $1.4 billion completed in 2024. And for 2026, the company is targeting a midpoint estimate for asset sales of $2.9 billion. This is an order to empower it to focus more on growth when it comes to its megacampuses.

By investing more heavily here, the firm hopes that it can improve overall profitability. This has been made possible by the resilience of its megacampus model, which has resulted in over 80% tenant retention over the last five years. Plus, the company is continuing to leverage standard cash rent escalators in order to boost revenue and profitability higher. In fact, 97% of its leases contain these. And with an average annual contractual rent escalator of about 3%, this should translate to between $50 million and $60 million of additional cash run income for the business each year. The fact that 91% of its leases fall under the triple net category is also a positive. This means that, in theory, overall upkeep costs should be very limited. Management is also pushing for certain corporate-related costs, specifically those involving general and administrative activities. The goal here is to cut expenses in 2025 and 2026 combined by $72 million on an annual run rate basis.

There are certain risk factors that could also impact the company in the near term. Historically, IHS funding had been pegged at between $47 billion and $48 billion a year. However, the current Presidential Administration in the U.S. is hostile to investments in public health initiatives or initiatives driven by public funds. The current proposal, for instance, is for a 40% cut to the NIH budget for the year. This would mean spending somewhere in the $27 billion range if it passes.

An estimated 1,500 layoffs at the NIH alone could be problematic, and the NIH had previously issued guidance capping indirect grant spending at 15%, which could ultimately impose some pain for parties that might be struggling to cover certain infrastructure costs. There has also been a reduction in life sciences spending in some areas. From 2023 to 2024, for instance, life science innovation spending from the biotech and pharmaceutical industries dropped from $317 billion to $293 billion. And after peaking at $41 billion in 2021, venture fundraising dedicated to the life sciences industry dropped to an estimated $9 billion in 2025.

The way I see it, these problems are largely transitory. A large and growing population, especially an aging population, should necessitate higher research and development costs in time. And considering that the U.S. health care space is estimated to be worth about $4.9 trillion, even small percentage growth over time can create significant upside. There is also the prospect of certain trade and other policies creating some onshoring activities. As the image above illustrates, there have already been major commitments when it comes to onshoring.

Based on management's own estimates, the midpoint for adjusted FFO should be around $1.53 billion for 2025 in its entirety. FFO should be around $1.35 billion. If we project out results based on what we have seen already for 2025, we would get adjusted operating cash flow of about $1.75 billion and EBITDA of $2.17 billion. For 2026, management expects the midpoint for FFO per share to translate to FFO of about $1.09 billion. Adjusted FFO would come in at about $1.24 billion, while adjusted operating cash flow would be $1.41 billion. This would also likely mean EBITDA of somewhere around $1.75 billion.

Using these estimates, and also using the historical results that the company achieved for the 2024 fiscal year, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. Honestly, these are very low multiples. Relative to other similar firms, however, the picture is a bit more nuanced. In the chart below, I compared it to 10 other similar firms. Five of them are more traditional office asset owners, while the others fall in the healthcare REIT space. Overall, the relative pricing here looks decent and is actually mostly good compared to the healthcare players. So that is a positive in and of itself.

It is worth noting that the valuation metrics that I looked at for 2026 factor in management's plan to reduce net debt by $1.7 billion. But because of the decline in overall profitability, we would be looking at an increase in the net leverage ratio for the company from an estimated 5.99 for 2025 to 6.45 for 2026. In the chart below, you can see what this means for the company in terms of how it stacks up against the other institutions that I compared it to already. Only one of the five traditional office REITs has a net leverage ratio lower than what Alexandria Real Estate Equities does. But when it comes to those that are focused on healthcare, four of them are better off.

As I mentioned at the start of this article, management will be announcing financial results for the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year after the market closes on January 26th. Leading up to that point, analysts believe that revenue will come in at $742.6 million. That would be down from the $788.9 million generated at the same time in 2024. They also expect a decline in overall profitability when it comes to adjusted FFO per share. But because of significant one-time events for the final quarter of 2024, earnings per share should improve. I would venture to say that most, if not all, cash flow metrics will also deteriorate year over year. So that should be monitored, as should any revised guidance that management comes up with.

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, it is my belief that perhaps I was a little too bullish about the company last year. Having said that, I don't believe that I am wrong to be bullish in general. There are some clear issues that the business has, but it also has advantages that other players in the space don't. The stock is cheap, and management is working to reduce net debt while also selling off non-core assets. And while the political climate might be hostile to the industry and while investments in the space are declining, the long-term demographic changes that we are experiencing must be positive enough to justify, when added to the aforementioned valuation of the company, a Buy rating.

data.title: Tesla Needs 20M FSD Subscribers Annually To Justify Its Valuation
data.description:
Tesla, Inc.'s (TSLA) share price is currently approximately at the same level as it was when I shared my bearish thesis in October. There were no large positive earnings surprises or notable positive earnings revisions since my previous call, meaning that TSLA remains substantially overvalued. The stock's fair value is far below its current share price, which means that TSLA remains a Strong Sell.

Tesla's rich valuation could have been justified if the power and number of positive catalysts were by far outweighing all the risks and uncertainties. However, it is not Tesla's case, as the company currently experiences not only intensifying competition but also unfavorable changes in commodity prices. Therefore, I expect Tesla's EPS growth to be limited not only due to the competition headwind [price wars] but also due to likely increasing costs per vehicle. All in all, Tesla's rich valuation ignores numerous negative factors.

Fundamentals and Q4 Earnings Preview

Tesla released its Q3 earnings release on October 22, and it was marked with a negative EPS surprise despite a $1+ billion revenue beat. Revenue increased by 11.6% on a YoY basis, which was the first quarter with double-digit growth since Q2 2023. While double-digit revenue growth in Q3 is a positive factor, it is important to understand that it was mostly triggered by a one-off event, which was the end of the $7,500 government tax credit. Another warning sign from Tesla's Q3 earnings release was the fact that the company's adjusted EPS declined from $0.72 to $0.50 on a YoY basis.

The upcoming earnings release is around the corner, as it is scheduled for January 28. Since I already explained that Tesla's Q3 revenue growth outlier was due to a one-off event, it is no wonder that Wall Street analysts expect the company's top-line dynamic to turn negative again in Q4. Revenue is expected to dip by 3.7% on a YoY basis, and such a forecast looks fair because of the challenging environment for the company. However, a much bigger warning sign for Tesla's investors is that the adjusted EPS is expected to drop much deeper than revenue, with a forecasted ~39% YoY decrease for Q4. Such a large gap between expected revenue and EPS decline means that Tesla's bottom line has experienced double pressure - both from revenue and cost sides.

The situation with revenue headwinds remains mostly unchanged over the last few years. Above all is the rapidly intensifying competition in the EV market. The Chinese EV giant BYD (BYDDF) is a huge competitive threat for Tesla, which I already mentioned in my previous TSLA coverage. I am not going to repeat myself, and this time I want to show that BYD is not the only significant competitive threat for Tesla from China. There is a famous group of much younger Chinese EV companies like Li Auto (LI), NIO (NIO), and XPeng (XPEV). If we look at the following comparative table, Tesla's forward projected revenue growth is not even comparable to these three Chinese players. They are also notably outpacing Tesla's revenue dynamic over the last three and five years.

Besides competitive threats from Chinese EV manufacturers, there are players from other countries gaining momentum. South Korean automotive giant Hyundai recently released its 2025 deliveries data, and their performance in the EV segment looks really impressive, which readers can see in the following table. And Hyundai's management is not going to rest on their laurels as they target to achieve 3.3 million annual EV [including hybrids] deliveries, which will be a big competitive challenge for Tesla. I would also like to spotlight the fact that Hyundai's EV sales surged by 49% in 2025, while Tesla has been losing its foothold in this market over the same period with a 28% decline.

Another big headwind for Tesla's revenue growth is the fact that interest rates remain quite high in the U.S., and the Fed plans only one rate cut in 2026. Therefore, it is difficult to expect a sharp revenue growth rebound in the domestic market as well. With rapidly intensifying competition across the world and high-interest rates in the U.S., there are significant risks of missing Q4 revenue estimates and the management sharing weak guidance for FY2026.

While revenue headwinds are not brand new, there are likely unfavorable catalysts from the cost side as well. Copper and lithium are two fundamental raw materials to build an EV, and prices for both these metals have been soaring recently. Copper prices (HG1:COM) hit an all-time high just a couple of weeks ago because investors forecast that there might be a shortage due to aggressively increasing demand from data centers. Since cloud giants plan to ramp up their AI infrastructure investments in 2026, and global EV sales are also growing, it is highly likely that copper prices will remain higher for longer. Furthermore, the following chart suggests that lithium prices (LC:COM) have been soaring since October, which is the beginning of Q4 2025.

With soaring prices for two key raw materials, it is not only Tesla's Q4 actual EPS under threat of missing consensus estimates, but it might also lead to a weaker-than-expected FY2026 EPS guidance.

Finally, Tesla's earnings surprise record is very weak for a $1+ trillion company. The company delivered four EPS misses in a row and a total of nine EPS misses over the last twelve quarters. The number of revenue misses over the last twelve quarters is slightly better: eight misses. With such a poor earnings surprise record together with multiple negative catalysts, delivering a dual beat next week looks like an extremely unlikely scenario.

Valuation Update

Considering all these negatives, Tesla's current valuation looks completely disconnected from its deteriorating fundamentals. I am not comparing Tesla's multiples to the sector median because the sector median is mostly represented by legacy automotive companies, while Tesla's mix of automotive, energy, and software should be in its own league. However, the company's current valuation ratios are mostly significantly higher than its own historical averages across the board. In my opinion, the one that screams overvaluation is the forward non-GAAP PEG ratio of 10.3, which is twice as high as the historical average.

Tesla's current $1.4 trillion valuation might be supported by its quite bullish long-term forward P/E ratio setup. Wall Street analysts expect the company's FY2025 P/E ratio of 264 to contract by almost three times by FY2028, which is a very impressive P/E ratio compression. However, the estimated P/E ratio for FY2028 of almost 90 is still extremely high.

Moreover, a projected approximately three-fold decline in Tesla's P/E ratio means that its EPS should almost triple between FY2025 and FY2028. With all the structural headwinds described for Tesla's core automotive business, such an aggressive EPS growth from this business appears to be highly unlikely. Tesla's TTM net income is approximately $5 billion, meaning that the company's FY2028 net income should be at least $15 billion ["at least" because there will likely be some dilution as well] to achieve a three-fold P/E contraction.

With all current headwinds for automotive businesses's revenue and costs, and likely remote prospects of robotaxis and humanoid robots to generate significant profits already in FY2028, the only more or less realistic net income boost can potentially come from the full self-drive [FSD] functionality. To reliably assess whether FSD can close the required $10 billion gap to achieve a three-fold P/E ratio compression, we can make quite simple calculations.

Tesla plans to generate ~$1,000 in revenue for annual FSD subscriptions per vehicle. Let's assume that it will be an unbelievably profitable business with a 50% net income margin. It implies $500 in assumed annual net income from FSD per vehicle. The above table shows that under this assumption, Tesla will need to sell 20 million FSD subscriptions per year by FY2028. Just a few months ago, there was information that Tesla sold approximately 8.5 million vehicles since inception. If we assume that Tesla continues selling approximately 2 million vehicles per year in 2026-2028, which is a quite optimistic assumption given intensifying competition, it will potentially have only 14.5 million cars sold since inception by the end of FY2028. Therefore, a scenario where Tesla's net income multiplies by 3x within just three years looks extremely unrealistic.

Risks Update

CEO Elon Musk's talent for persuading investors in his groundbreaking ideas during earnings calls is Tesla's powerful weapon. As I said earlier, Tesla's earnings surprise record is extremely weak, with substantially more revenue and EPS misses than beats over the three years [12 quarters]. Nevertheless, Tesla remains a company with a $1.4 trillion market cap, and its share price is currently higher by approximately four times compared to January 2023. It means that Tesla's investors frequently ignore earnings misses and prefer to focus more on Elon Musk's bold long-term plans shared during earnings calls. Therefore, even if Tesla delivers another disappointing quarter from the headline numbers perspective, it might mean nothing to investors or to the share price.

In particular, investors might become much more bullish if Elon Musk unveils something exciting about the FSD feature or a roadmap of penetrating the thriving robotaxi market. Promises around these two businesses were quite powerful catalysts during a few previous earnings calls, and history might repeat itself. Another piece of information that can be absorbed with big optimism might be plans for expanding Tesla's portfolio of EV models. If the company provides concrete plans about expansion into the budget segment, it might also be a powerful catalyst because the cheapest models of BYD, like the Seagull or Qin Plus [both starting below $20,000 per car], currently do not face direct competition from Tesla.

Bottom Line

To conclude, I believe Tesla remains a Strong Sell. The company is surrounded by numerous negative catalysts into the Q4 earnings season, and the stock is extremely overvalued.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Denison Mines: De-Risking And The New Phoenix ISR Milestone
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Dev Shroff as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

I rate Denison Mines Corp. (DNN) a BUY with a price target of $4.18 within 3-6 months, conditional on the approval of the CNSC federal license for the Wheeler River project. With the de-risking enabled by the CNSC license, the target discount rate is 8%. Denison Mines specializes in uranium mining, has 897,186,541 shares outstanding, and the stock has traded between a 52 week range of $1.08 to $3.72.

This investment thesis is based on a variety of factors, from Denison Mines’ Phoenix ISR project in the Wheeler Basin, which is expected to rank in the lowest quartile globally by all-in cost per kilogram produced, to their strong balance sheet and rising cost of U₃O₈ (triuranium octoxide), a major part of powering nuclear plants responsible for supplying energy to AI data centers.

Though Denison Mines is a Canadian company, all values in this article are in USD.

All company financials are current as of the Q3 2025 report. Project costs and other related values are more current, specified by their relevant sources. This report was prepared on conditions mainly observed between January 7th and January 11th of 2026. Since then, the factors in consideration, especially uranium prices, have only become stronger, so it does not seem to be the case that the rating should face any changes, at least in the negative direction.

Company Summary

Denison Mines is a uranium mining and exploration company with interests in many mining projects throughout the Athabasca Basin in Saskatchewan, Canada. Their flagship project, Wheeler River (95% interest), features the first-of-its-kind ISR (in-situ recovery) mining project in the Athabasca region. This project revolves around the Phoenix deposit, which uses ISR, and the Gryphon deposit, a traditional mining operation.

Operations

To cover the current main corporate operations of Denison Mines, we must cover the ISR and freeze wall technologies, and understand how Denison Mines will fund their flagship project. The first phase of the Wheeler River project is the Phoenix ISR project. The planned implementation of ISR technology in the Athabasca Basin gives the Phoenix project a respectable pre-tax All-in Cost of $16.04/lb of U₃O₈ extracted, placing it in the lowest quartile of production costs globally.

Due to Denison Mines' flagship project being an ISR project rather than a conventional mine, their main focus is more aligned with that of a fluid dynamics corporation rather than a large-scale excavation corporation.

Other mines in the Athabasca region, like those of Denison Mines' competitors, have risks from water inflows that can shut down production for months, as was the case with McArthur River in 2008 and others. The freeze wall technology Denison Mines intends to utilize for the Phoenix project created a barrier blocking inflows, mitigating this risk almost entirely. In my view, the market is not fully pricing in the value of the operational reliability that comes from this. If Denison Mines successfully applies ISR to Phoenix, they could very well apply it to at least several other projects in their vast portfolio, creating above industry-average margins for decades.

The freeze wall method is meant to isolate the body of uranium ore from the water table and the surrounding area, protecting the environment and the mine. However, this is an innovation in the mining space and is expected to reduce technical complexity, initial capital cost, and environmental impact. Although this does mean there is a non-zero risk of failure, as with any innovation.

To fund this flagship project, Denison Mines has approximately $368.04 million worth of net non-uranium assets, along with approximately $155.80 million worth of physical U₃O₈ (triuranium octoxide). They plan on utilizing many of these resources to reduce risk in financing the project.

Liquidity and Financing

As mentioned before, Denison Mines has approximately $368.04 million worth of net non-uranium assets, along with $155.80 million worth of physical U₃O₈. This directly supports the approximately $436.08 million initial capital costs for the Phoenix ISR project.

Assets and Liabilities

It is worth mentioning, however, that of the $368.04 million net non-uranium assets, cash equivalents of approximately $339.23 million contribute to the assets, and a Convertible note-Embedded Derivative liability of approximately $218.60 million. This liability is subject to change with the share price. For a more in-depth dive on the major assets, Denison Mines also holds approximately $201.98 million in property, plant, and equipment, and approximately $155.80 million of physical U₃O₈ mentioned previously. Denison Mines also holds $35.08 million in capped call options. Holding these options helps offset dilution up to a share price of $4.32.

Internal Growth Factors

Very recently, on January 8, 2026, DNN announced that Wheeler River would be able to receive grid power. Compared to diesel generation, this significantly reduces both operational energy costs and carbon intensity. Besides the flagship Phoenix ISR project at Wheeler River, the Gryphon deposit acts as a gateway into the next phase of production in the next few decades.

A similar case can be seen with the Midwest (MWJV) project. Both of these projects show that a major fall-off in production for the next few decades is very unlikely, assuming production starts for Phoenix. In addition, as Denison Mines is still mainly in the exploration stage as a company, many other projects, such as Moon Lake and Sabre, show potential to prove themselves as major cash flow generating assets in the coming years. Although these projects are not necessarily as large as those similar to the scale of Phoenix or the Gryphon deposit, they are major free upside that is not considered due to their lack of complete feasibility and extraction plans.

External Growth Factors

A major factor pushing up uranium prices, and Denison Mines’ share price by association, is the absolutely massive AI data center power demand. Major AI companies across North America are scrambling to construct new data centers. Nuclear is a 24/7 carbon-free energy source perfect for this ever-increasing demand. U₃O₈, the primary ore extracted by Denison Mines, happens to be primarily used for nuclear power generation. In addition, Tier-1 uranium mines like Cigar Lake, owned by Denison Mines’ competitors, are expected to deplete into the mid-2030s, creating a gap for Denison Mines to fill demand if they continue expanding their range of projects that break ground and successfully produce U₃O₈. Lastly, a short mention on geopolitical security. A significant portion of the world's uranium is produced in the Russia-Kazakh corridor. In a time of rising geopolitical tensions, increasing U₃O₈ production in the western hemisphere is only a benefit.

Binary De-Risking

The primary condition leading to the $4.18 valuation is the approval of the CNSC Federal License for the Wheeler River, and in particular, the Phoenix ISR project. This is expected to be given to Denison Mines in early 2026, after their hearing in late 2025. This approval removes a major binary risk and allows Denison Mines to truly transition from a mostly exploratory company to a major producer of U₃O₈. For the purposes of this valuation, Denison Mines becoming a major producer is considered to move it from a higher WACC to 8%, in line with its competitors like Cameco Corporation (CCJ).

Risks

It is important to acknowledge risks to Denison Mines’ bottom line and future earnings potential.

Firstly, if the CNSC license is not approved or delayed, this will disrupt the planned progress of the Phoenix ISR project, significantly reducing the earnings potential for Denison Mines in the time it would take them to reapply and fix the issues causing a non-issuance.

Second, the freeze wall technology, while proven at small scales, is still a new innovation in the way it is being applied to the Phoenix ISR project. If the freeze wall were to fail during construction or early operation before significant portions of uranium ore are extracted, it would be devastating to the net value of the Phoenix mine and the nearby environment.

Lastly, a major risk to the entire business model that Denison Mines has built is the price of uranium. The value of the corporation and its stock is intertwined at a high level, from both its physical holdings to the profits it generates by selling the ore. If uranium demand and thus its price were to crash, Denison Mines would likely follow suit.

DCF and Valuation

For an in-depth view of how the following values are calculated, please see the spreadsheet at the end. This will be a summary of the values derived in the DCF and the conclusions reached using them. The first section will cover Denison Mines’ own value, and the second will adjust that estimation in comparison to its peers.

Intrinsic Value

The after-tax NPV of the following assets are calculated using an 8% discount rate, which assumes the CNSC License is approved, and that binary risk is eliminated. For the Phoenix ISR, the after-tax NPV is $1,257.10 million, for the Gryphon deposit, it is $591.86 million, and for the MWJW project, it is $175.10 million. This covers the major projects accounting for most of the company’s value. The Phoenix ISR NPV was calculated accounting for the Saskatchewan Royalty on uranium, Canadian corporate income taxes, and writing off taxable income with initial startup costs.

Adding all of these to the $155.80 million of held physical uranium and $368.04 million in net non-uranium assets gives a total company value of $2,547.90 million. Dividing this by the shares outstanding gives an estimated current share intrinsic base case value of $2.84, with respect to the uranium spot price of approximately $82/lb as of January 7, 2025. Please note that this is close to the most conservative estimate of Denison Mines’ intrinsic value.

So far, the valuation does not account for tax-deferred assets, many other projects, scarcity premium, or uranium price growth. Denison Mines is estimated to have at least $200.00 million in historical CEE losses and other tax-deferred assets. They also own 22.5% of the McClean Lake Mill operated by Orano, which currently produces a small amount of toll revenue for Denison Mines. However, this is the only licensed uranium mill in the world designed to process high grade uranium without dilution, according to a Denison Mines press release. It would be reasonable to assume that with rising uranium demand, an asset like this would command a 25% premium, adding an additional $44.00 million to the NPV of Denison Mines’ assets. Denison Mines also has a multitude of other properties yet to be fully established or explored, some of which, like Moon Lake and Waterbury Lake, show great promise, reasonably adding $150.00 million to their assets’ NPV.

This would bring Denison Mines’ total NPV of assets to approximately $2,941.90 million, translating to a share price of $3.28.

Peer Benchmarking

There are many uranium mining competitors globally, but we will look at two operating in Denison Mines’ own backyard: Cameco and NexGen Energy Ltd. (NXE).

Cameco is a benchmark for a Tier-1 mining producer. They hold major projects, including Cigar Lake and McArthur River, which are very established conventional mines. They do have low costs, but they face many complexities in operations that an ISR approach like that of Denison Mines would avoid.

NexGen Energy is a Canadian uranium exploration and mining company just like Denison Mines. Their Rook 1 project is proposed to be a massive mine exploring one of the largest deposits in Canada. They would face lower operating costs for the project because the grade of uranium ore is high, but they would also suffer from the higher initial capital expenditures, estimated to be $1,580 million, of a conventional mine. This allows Denison Mines to frame itself as a more agile mining operation company. The lower initial capital expenditures for the flagship project, less than half of the Rook 1 project, allow Denison Mines to be slightly more flexible with the projects they start developing.

Compared to both Cameco and NexGen, which operate conventional mines, there are a few specific advantages to ISR projects. Due to the higher margins that result from a lower expenditure method like ISR, the IRR of projects utilizing the method is significantly higher. For example, one of Cameco’s largest projects is Cigar Lake, which in 2023 was reported to have an IRR of 8.3%. Compare this to Denison Mines’ flagship Phoenix project in the Wheeler Basin, with an IRR estimated to be a rarely seen 105.9%. That is over an order of magnitude difference, and such strong margins enable Denison Mines to stay profitable even in relatively major upsets to uranium prices. This IRR on the Phoenix project is partly due to how ISR works. Denison Mines only has to create small holes to inject solvents to dissolve the minerals and pump them to the surface, instead of the large-scale excavation needed for traditional mines.

For Denison Mines, this translates to a benchmark that they can achieve if the binary risk of CNSC license is achieved. If they become a Tier-1 producer like Cameco, they can command a similar estimated P/NAV of 1.5x. Due to Denison Mines being in the very early stages and not reaching production for a few years, even with the license, but with potentially better technology, we can assume a P/NAV of at least 1.15x, bringing the estimated share value to $3.77.

Sensitivity and External Variations

The price of U₃O₈ has a very large effect on Denison Mines' overall value. In the spreadsheet below, you can find a sensitivity table covering the base case intrinsic value of Denison Mines varying with uranium spot pricing. Taking an excerpt from that table, the base case intrinsic value if uranium stays at its current spot price of approximately $82/lb, the base case intrinsic value per share would be $2.84, as mentioned previously.

However, if uranium prices increase steadily as they have been since the bottom at the end of Q1 2025, they could very likely hit $96/lb in the coming fiscal year, if not by the end of Q1 2026. The start of the current bull run of uranium spot prices began just around March 31, 2025. We can track this with the U-U.TO ticker (tracking the Sprott Physical Uranium Trust Fund).

The price closed at $13.20 on March 31, 2025. As of January 21, 2026, the price at 10 am CST was around $22.14. Given that there has been a relatively steady rise since March 31, 2025, and that 296 days have passed since then, if we create a linear regression line between now and then, the line would have a slope of around 0.002288 or a daily average increase of 0.229% in price. If we assume a similar trend to the end of March 2026 (because that is near the end of Q1, the same time Denison Mines should have received CNSC approval by), the uranium spot price could increase by 16.02% compared to January 21, 2026. This increase in stock price would correspond with a rise in per-pound uranium cost of approximately $13.13, bringing the total spot price to $95.13/lb. If this happens, the intrinsic value base case per share would rise to $3.20.

This corresponds to the high planned growth in data centers in the United States alone. According to Cleanview, as of January 2026, the US has 14,167 MW of capacity in data centers with over 155,403 MW in additional capacity to be added in planned projects. With over an order of magnitude growth in capacity planned, and nuclear power planned to power more and more of these, a $95.13/lb uranium spot price is very reasonable. Incorporating the higher uranium price into the valuation estimation made by adding other assets ($394.00 million) not valued in the intrinsic base case, as shown in the DCF, and multiplying by the 1.15x P/NAV from peer benchmarking, would translate to a total valuation of $4.18. This presents significant upside untapped in the stock price, of approximately $3.70 as of January 21, 2026, at 10 am CST, of almost 13%.

This considers a time frame matched with CNSC licensure for the Phoenix ISR project. On a longer time frame, however, a similar consistent trend in the explosion of power demand from the AI data centers could push uranium to all-time highs, with a reasonable likelihood of achieving a spot price of $110/lb within around a year. This would allow the Phoenix project alone to add around $685.00 million to Denison Mines net assets, and around $0.76 to their share price. A trend like this would allow for uncapped upside, easily pushing the share price beyond $4.7.

Future Growth

With Denison Mines currently trading on the NYSE for around $3.70, you could argue that the upside of Wheeler River is priced in at a 70-80% probability of success. This does not, however, account for the discounted valuation rates of a uranium producer. The market could have accounted for the NPV of Wheeler River, which would be accessible with the CNSC license; however, the current trading price is nowhere near where it would be if Denison Mines' stock fully adopted a P/NAV of 1.3x-1.5x, similar to those of large-scale producers.

If the optimistic trend ($110/lb uranium spot price) from the previous section were to come true in the order of around a year, a P/NAV of even 1.3x would send Denison Mines share price soaring to over $5.3. This is a significant upside not captured by the market due to incomplete confidence in uranium prices. This leaves significant chances for a larger profit potential if uranium price trends are stronger than expected in the positive direction.

Lastly, the shorter-term valuation of $4.18 should be considered a logical target base case, which is likely to occur within a relatively short time frame of 3-6 months, corresponding with CNSC licensure. There are several factors outside of the company's own reach that affect its share price, other than uranium spot pricing. Given that Denison Mines holds the only advanced ISR project in the entire Athabasca Basin, it should not only command a scarcity premium but also be a prime target to be acquired by larger companies like Cameco, who would benefit greatly by adding a low-cost, high-margin project like Phoenix to their portfolio.

data.title: Celestica Stock: Why Alphabet Looking Elsewhere Is Actually A Good Thing
data.description:
Celestica Inc. (CLS) shares have had an outstanding 12-month run as AI demand has sent operating results higher, but the stock is dropping in Thursday trading after a report that Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), aka Google, could expand some of its tensor processing unit, or TPU, business to other suppliers. While this is a scary headline, I think this will be a short-term hiccup of which investors could benefit from taking advantage.

After yo-yoing around the last few days amidst the Trump administration's renewed tariff threats, CLS had regained much of its lost ground until a report came out Thursday from Digitimes claiming that Google could be adding suppliers for its TPU server deployments as it expects demand to ramp up in the coming years. This comes in the wake of Google's announcement of Ironwood, its 7th-generation TPU, which will be specialized for AI inference workloads as the company bets that the ASIC era is imminent. I have been bullish on ASICs and their benefits over GPUs in some valuable use cases (you can read my recent bearish article on Nvidia (NVDA) here) to the benefit of companies like Google and Celestica.

However, the market seems to be panicking about this report, perhaps concerned that Google is looking to move away from Celestica and shift existing business elsewhere. So should investors be worried about this development and its potential impact on the latter's future earnings prospects? Read on for my answer!

One thing we've seen again and again the last few years as AI has taken the global economy by storm is that demand is outstripping supply at an enormous rate throughout every layer of the sector. Micron (MU) is seeing its stock explode as memory demand for AI infrastructure build-outs has created a massive supply crunch that has customers scrambling for any memory they can find from any supplier they can find, Microsoft (MSFT), Meta Platforms (META), and other hyperscalers have signed large deals with third-party neocloud operators like Nebius (NBIS) and CoreWeave (CRWV) to provide GPU compute for internal workloads as new data center construction can't keep up with demand, and Nvidia is selling every GPU they can possibly manufacture for exorbitant prices and yet it's still not enough to satisfy demand as Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) and others have seen their market share grow.

Truly, we are in the age of capacity constraint, and everyone is racing to expand in order to snap up more of the AI pie. Celestica is no different—Google's search for additional suppliers is likely not an indicator that Celestica will be receiving less business, but rather that Google is expecting demand greater than Celestica can satisfy alone. The company has committed to expanding capacity and its manufacturing footprint, but ASIC demand is increasing rapidly enough that Celestica cannot keep up. And it's not just Google. Broadcom (AVGO) and Marvell (MRVL) are also seeing demand for their XPU ASICs pick up substantially, putting additional strain on Celestica's output.

Contrary to prevailing sentiment, this is a good thing. Companies left and right are all racing to expand capacity, which is tanking their margins and leaving them exposed to a potential pullback in demand. One such example that I bring up often is CoreWeave—the company has amassed a significant debt pile relative to its revenue and market cap as it has aggressively expanded its data centers. If the AI bubble pops or pulls back, CoreWeave could find itself in a pickle.

Celestica is run much more conservatively, which results in two of my favorite words when analyzing a stock: operating leverage.

Celestica runs lightweight and expands slowly to keep pace with demand instead of rushing to lay down track in front of a speeding train, which keeps margins healthy and is a major hallmark of a company that is being optimized for long-term success.

So to answer my original question, I don't think investors should worry about Google searching for additional suppliers. The increase in demand and shipments for TPU servers will provide better margins for suppliers and will sustain additional revenue and net income growth as these infrastructure build-outs ramp. We could see additional volatility in the short term as the market digests this news, but I think CLS is a long-term winner, and I am rating it a Buy.

Thanks for reading!

data.title: BioCryst: The $700 Million Pivot From Oral Convenience To Injectable
data.description:
BioCryst Pharmaceuticals’ stock (BCRX) has slumped 20% since my last look in July 2025. This is despite the broader biotech market (XBI) rallying.

The company markets ORLADEYO, which is an oral, prophylactic hereditary angioedema (HAE) drug. ORLADEYO’s biggest moat is in its convenience, but this is being pressured by longer-acting injectables and new orals.

So, although I thought some kind of discount was warranted in its stock, its relatively low enterprise value (compared to ORLADEYO revenues) did not scream “expensive” to me. So I was neutral on BioCryst.

The following article looks for the reasons for BioCryst’s recent struggles.

Recent Developments

I'm going to start by catching up on ORLADEYO’s commercial execution and guidance.

In the third quarter, ORLADEYO revenue was $159.1 million. This is a 37% year-over-year increase. Operating income was $29.6 million, and net income was $12.9 million. Third-quarter results prompted the company to raise its full-year 2025 ORLADEYO revenue guidance to $590 million-$600 million.

Earlier this month, the company provided preliminary full-year 2025 results. ORLADEYO's net revenue came in at $601 million (marginally above guidance), up 37% year over year. In the fourth quarter, ORLADEYO’s revenue was $151 million. The company guided ORLADEYO's net revenue in 2026 between $625 million and $645 million.

To retire the remaining Pharmakon term debts, BioCryst completed the sale of its European ORLADEYO business. The company ended 2025 with $338 million in cash and investments.

BioCryst has been busy recently diversifying its business.

In October 2025, the company signed an agreement to acquire Astria for an implied enterprise value of around $700 million. The deal adds an injectable long-acting monoclonal antibody inhibitor of plasma kallikrein, STAR-0215, which is in a Phase 3 trial for HAE prophylaxis. The hope is that the asset will allow for every three-month and six-month administration. The company expects pivotal ALPHA-ORBIT data in early 2027.

The deal comes after a flurry of newer, longer-interval prevention options. ANDEMBRY, a factor XIIa monoclonal antibody, was FDA-approved in June 2025. It allows monthly subcutaneous dosing. Elsewhere, DAWNZERA, an anti-sense inhibitor of prekallikrein, was FDA-approved two months later and allows dosing every four weeks or every eight weeks.

The market also welcomed a new oral on-demand drug in KalVista's (KALV) EKTERLY last July. Meanwhile, Pharvaris’ (PHVS) oral deucrictibant is prepping for an NDA submission this year in on-demand, with topline Phase 3 prophylaxis data (CHAPTER-3) expected in the third quarter of 2026.

BioCryst is also diversifying its pipeline outside of HAE.

The company reported that a Phase 1 trial of BCX17725 is enrolling healthy volunteers and patients (Netherton syndrome) with initial data expected by the end of the first quarter. And its diabetic macular edema asset (avoralstat, a plasma kallikrein inhibitor) is enrolling patients with initial Phase 1 data expected later this year. However, judging by recent management commentary, this asset is already being deprioritized. It intends to out-license its DME asset, and data may only be shown when a deal materializes.

Circling back to its Netherton syndrome asset, and I think I've touched on this before, there is some uncertainty regarding disease prevalence. For instance, there is no ICD-10 code. Instead, diagnosis relies on proxy signals like “bamboo hair.” So the true addressable population is not well-defined ahead of Phase 1 BCX17725 clinical data.

The company is making it no secret (“it's not the same BioCryst that you've seen before”) that it intends to rebrand from a development-stage biotech to a cash-generative, rare disease operator with business development as the next growth engine. This is a nice way of saying that it can no longer depend on one product to inspire growth. The walls are closing in on ORLADEYO, and the company is doing what is needed (with limited resources) to build a ladder.

This is the challenge of single-asset pharma companies. Patents expire and technology advances, requiring continuous innovation to stay afloat. The issue with BioCryst is that it never really generated a lot of profits from ORLADEYO. And innovation has been slow and mostly bought at a premium (e.g., Astria).

So moving forward, I suspect that much of BioCryst’s valuation is now dependent on STAR-0215 developments. Compared to other options in HAE prophylaxis, this asset offers an incremental improvement in dosing interval.

If successful in Phase 3, whether or not this translates into a blockbuster opportunity remains unclear.

Final Words

Despite its US approval in 2020, ORLADEYO has merely kept the company at bay. Over the last five years, BioCryst’s stock is off 25%. And the company's recent acquisition of Astria looks more like a last-ditch effort than it does a forward-looking strategy.

In any event, it is challenging to be decisively bearish on the stock given its market capitalization of just $1.4 billion. Moreover, I do think the Astria acquisition (better late than never) could pay off handsomely if the company executes “to the T.”

So I'm willing to maintain a “Hold” rating on BioCryst’s stock despite management’s historical inability to create shareholder value. But it reads more like “hold off on buying” than anything else.

data.title: PBW: Clean Breakout With Clean Energy, Pure Valuation (Upgrade)
data.description:
Clean energy stocks are printing fresh multi-year highs after what has been a very volatile stretch regarding macroeconomic developments. I had a “Hold” rating on the Invesco WilderHill Clean Energy ETF (PBW) back in September 2024.

Shares went on to plunge into the Liberation Day low last April but have since been on a heater. Overall, the ETF has returned 95% over the past 16 months, outperforming the S&P 500 by about 70 percentage points.

Today, I’m upgrading PBW to a “Buy.” With the fund hitting levels not seen since 2023 and a solid valuation, I expect more upward momentum heading into the rest of 2026.

PBW: Major Alpha YoY

According to the issuer, PBW is based on the WilderHill Clean Energy Index. “The fund will normally invest at least 90% of its total assets in common stocks that comprise the index. The fund invests in stocks of companies operating across energy, utilities, alternative energy resources, independent power and renewable electricity producers, and renewable electricity sectors. The fund and the index are rebalanced and reconstituted quarterly.”

PBW is a small ETF, with just $798 million in assets under management as of Jan. 21, 2026. That is up from only $280 million at the time of my previous assessment. The annual expense ratio is moderate to elevated at 64 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is low at about 0.7%. PBW is now ranked No. 1 out of 17 in its Sub Class.

Share-price momentum is very strong right now, earning the product a pristine A+ ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. PBW can be a risky fund, though, as evidenced by its lofty historical standard deviation readings and a niche industry thematic allocation. Still, liquidity is decent, given average daily volume just shy of 1 million shares. The median 30-day bid/ask spread can turn wide, but it’s currently 9 basis points, per Invesco.

For an update on the portfolio, the 1-star, Neutral-rated ETF by Morningstar plots along the very bottom row of the style box, indicating its bent to global small caps. Just 7% of the fund is invested in large caps, while mid-cap access is 10%. There is, however, a solid mix of value, blend, and growth. What’s more, diversification is seen via its geographical breakdown (18% ex-U.S.).

On valuation, PBW trades a smidgen under 20x earnings. Pair that with a long-term EPS growth rate of 23%, and the resulting PEG ratio is compelling, under 1.

PBW: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Digging into the sector view, Industrials commands a significant (though not extreme) 33% of PBW. Information Technology is 24%, though that is primarily related to semiconductor/solar names, which are more cyclically oriented than traditional diversified technology megacaps.

The modified equal-weight product has a low total allocation to the top 10 stocks, helping to limit single-stock risk.

PBW: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, we are currently in the throes of a neutral stretch. Over the past 10 years, January and February have produced tepid returns, on average. Then, March and April have been decidedly bearish. So, this is not a constructive indicator, admittedly.

PBW: Neutral Near-Term Calendar Trends

The Technical Take

With a compelling valuation and slightly negative seasonals, PBW’s technical situation is constructive. Notice in the chart below that shares broke out from a steep downtrend from the January 2021 peak. The move—which occurred during the middle of the last year—appears to have been merely the start of a major upside reversal.

Today, PBW may close at its highest mark in almost three years. I see near-term resistance near the $39 mark—the measured move upside price target based on the depth of the 2024-2025 decline and subsequent breakout. Longer term, perhaps PBW can ascend to $65-$70.

Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. It has ranged in a bullish zone between 40 and 70 since the second quarter of last year. Moreover, there was a high amount of shares traded as PBW rallied.

The Q4 dip turned out to be a correction in the trend of larger degree (higher), and there is now a high amount of volume by price below today’s spot (bullish). Support is in the mid-$20s (where the 200dma and November 2025 low coincide).

PBW: Long-Term Breakout, Bullish RSI, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a “Buy” rating on PBW. It sports absolute and relative strength, and with an attractive valuation, I see higher prices for this clean energy ETF as 2026 progresses.

data.title: Are Prediction Markets Really A Threat To DraftKings?
data.description:
Over the last year or so, prediction markets have gained attention as a potential alternative to traditional sportsbooks.

Smooth onboarding processes, a wide variety of markets, and availability in all 50 states have attracted customers of all stripes, and the growth in top exchanges like Kalshi and Polymarket has been immense.

Naturally, this has sportsbook investors worried, and stocks in top betting companies like Flutter Entertainment (FLUT) and DraftKings (DKNG) have fallen in recent quarters.

At first glance, these new products appear disruptive, but as I'll lay out in this article today, I don’t believe prediction markets are a material threat to the long-term health and viability of sportsbook businesses.

As such, DraftKings - a stock that has been punished over the last year - appears unbelievably well priced for future growth.

I've covered the company twice before here on Seeking Alpha, but I believe the value proposition here is stronger than ever. As a result, I'm reiterating my 'Strong Buy' rating.

Today, I’ll make the case that prediction markets are not a material threat to DraftKings and explain why I see DKNG as an excellent opportunity in the present market landscape.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

The Prediction Market Threat

If you’ve been tuned into the news flow around betting companies over the last year, you’ve likely heard about prediction markets and the threat they pose to traditional sportsbooks.

With simple yes/no event contracts, quick payouts, simple onboarding, and fewer regulatory hurdles, event contract platforms like Kalshi have quickly built a serious book of business.

Not only that, but exchanges offer markets where traders can trade against one another. As such, platform revenue and profitability are directly tied to overall betting volumes and not specific customer outcomes. Obviously, this is a less risky business model than traditional sportsbooks, which both offer and make the markets that users place wagers in.

It's no surprise that investors see prediction contracts as a threat.

Recently, on an earnings call, DKNG management had the following to say about prediction markets, commenting on DraftKings’ advantages when it comes to pricing and user experience (emphasis mine):

We have experienced numerous waves of competition in recent years...

By comparison, predictions are structurally limited, lacking the depth and breadth of a sports betting offering...

I think parlays are just challenging with predictions because it's liquidity based. So you can do some prepackaged stuff, and I think that could work. But to be able to really have a parlay offering that looks anything like what you'd find in an online sportsbook just isn't possible...

If you remember, for us, the reason we have higher margins is... because we have a very strong bet mix. Our singles hold is actually nowhere near what our actual hold rate is. Our singles hold is in the mid-single digits. So that's sort of the comparison point if you're thinking about predictions.

Now on predictions, you have both the fees that the exchange is taking as well as the other fees that are collected from the FCM or anyone else in the ecosystem as well as the spreads that the market makers are taking. So there's a lot more mouth[s] to feed in the ecosystem versus, in our case, we are the market maker solely on our online sportsbook... So really, it makes it that we have more margin to play with there...

The other thing is even if there are cases where there's better pricing, promotions don't really work in the same way in predictions as they do in OSB. So we can give back money and help create cool profit boost and other kinds of promotions for customers that engage them, which really isn't something that predictions is doing.

As you can clearly see in these comments, DraftKings management does not see prediction markets as a significant threat due to the fact that platforms like Kalshi offer ostensibly worse pricing and significantly less flexibility when it comes to incentives and unique bet construction.

While the arguments here make sense, I wanted to test live markets to see whether or not the math holds true.

First, I took a look at pricing for the upcoming NFC Championship game.

On DraftKings, betting the Rams moneyline gets you odds of +130, meaning that a $1,000 wager equals a $2,300 payout:

On Kalshi, the market was priced at roughly the same odds, but the payout for a $1,000 trade was $64 less at $2,236. This is due to the fees that the exchange takes, which vary by the size of your trade:

Then I tested a parlay trade on DraftKings and Kalshi.
